sentences.text: Yes.
We met at the-- when we were hiding out in the forest when-- this was in 1940- 1943, in summer.
He came there with a friend.
And he came from a different camp.
This was the time when I was with my cousin and this group.
And this is how we met.
And we were just there together, the whole group, until, until, until the end, you know-- whatever we did, you know, together-- running together, hiding, you know, until the end.
So--
Yeah.
Then we came to Lwow.
And there was-- like I said before-- there was no place to go.
And he was very anxious to go back to his city.
And this was quite a distance.
And-- but he was very determined to go and see if some of his relatives-- because he came from a very, very big family-- if some of his par-- I mean, some of his relatives.
And his father was there.
He went back there.
And one of his brother.
So unfortunately, he came there.
He didn't find anybody.
Lancut.
And then he, he was there, I think, a, a few days.
He talked to some neighbors, to some, you know, Gentile people, who, who-- they were sort of befriended.
And he got, he got a ID-- you know, identification-- because we had nothing that time.
No, no.
We were--
I was, I was left in Poland-- I mean, in Lemberg.
I was just there with the rest of the friends.
And he said that he's going.
He's going to his city.
And he'll be back as soon as possible.
And he hijacked, you know, with, with a lot of [INAUDIBLE] these soldiers who were going, you know, who were--
I mean, you know, he was on the road.
I'm sorry.
No, I'm sorry.
I should-- this shouldn't be maybe mentioned.
I didn't mean hijacked.
I, I meant like he asked for, for for transportation, for, for, for a ride.
That's what I meant-- can this be-- OK.
And then he went to the city hall.
He got a, a ID that he can come back.
Because was this already Poland.
See, Lwow was Russia.
And then I think [INAUDIBLE].
And [INAUDIBLE] was Poland.
And then, yeah, he came back as soon as he could.
I can't give you the days.
But-- and then he tried to talk me into go there.
Because we weren't married.
We were just, you know, girl and boyfriend.
And I, I just had no place to go in Lwow.
There were a few friends.
But these other friends were left with a sister, with a brother, with, with parents.
But I had just had nobody.
And I was very friendly with them.
But still, you know, I was like the fifth, fifth wheel.
So I decided to go with him.
And it was a journey also.
I just didn't go like that.
But you know, there were some Russian, Jewish, Jew, Jewish Russians, who came.
And one was a invalid and with a wife, with a mother-in-law and this.
And he wanted to get away from Russia.
He wanted to go to, to the Polish side.
And he encouraged-- we met there in this place in Lwow.
And he encouraged my husband, you know, that to go back.
And he's-- he wants to go with us.
And he wants us to lead them someplace to, to get there.
So anyway, because of him, we went together.
And we just also, you know, got on a, on a train station.
And it was difficult to go.
I mean, on the Russian side, it was OK.
But then when we came to the border in Przemysl that, this was the border between Russia and then Poland, they wouldn't let through, you know, people without identifications.
And my husband had already identification that he has from, from the other side.
But I and these people didn't have.
So it was an ordeal.
We had to stay overnight.
And during the night, you know, when they changed trains, you know, on, in Przemysl, these were the Russian trains.
And then the other side were Polish trains.
And these were not regular trains.
They were like freight trains.
So we sort of like-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we just took a chance, you know.
We got on a, on the other side and during the night.
And we got on a Polish train.
And we went till, till Lancut, till [INAUDIBLE] and there till the city where my husband was from.
And then we came there.
So there, he felt already like free because it was, as you know, a city that, you know, his parents, his grandfather, was very known there.
And he was even part to the city hall, his grandfather and this.
And so we got into a house which was a Jewish house.
But Gentiles lived there.
But they gave us one room to stay there.
And then there was another house that was a Jew, a Jewish family, very nice, almost like a villa.
And then they made there this house, like whoever comes back from this city that would be able to stay there for the time being.
So we stayed there for a few months till, I guess, until-- because the Russians-- oh, let's see-- the Russians went that time till, till [INAUDIBLE], I think.
And the other side, Krakow, was still under German.
This was during winter time.
Yeah, they stopped for a few months during winter time, I think.
So-- you know, it's so difficult.
Which year was it?
This This was in '40-- '44, beginning for '45, beginning of '45.
Because when they attacked again, the last, the attack attack, I think, was in May or something.
The Germans when they, when they really-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yeah was end of, end of '44, beginning of '45.
Yeah, so we lived in Lancut.
What did you do while you were in Lancut.
Nothing, just lived-- stayed there and tried to get by, to have what to eat and little-- that place where we stayed, they understand.
Pardon me?
My husband managed.
He used to go to, to, to the Gentile people who knew his parents and this.
And there was no money to buy.
I don't, I don't remember having money.
But somehow, he got little bits here and there.
And we pulled through beginning of '45.
And then, then-- of course, it started like the, the, the, the beginning of the end of the Germans.
They started again with the fight, I think.
And, and I don't remember when the-- anyhow, they-- so then they took over Krakow.
And then we move to Krakow.
The Pol-- the, the, the I mean, I guess the Russians.
It was Poland.
But the Russians were still fighting there.
But they stopped for a few months between '44 and '45.
And then I think the [INAUDIBLE] spring.
I think they started again the fight.
And then when Krakow was freed, again, this, this man who took me that time out from ghetto, from, from the hiding to the ghetto, he came by to the city, to Lancut.
Because he was already that time, you know, functioning.
And he was going to Krakow.
So he stopped over, because this was like in between Lwow and Krakow.
And he stopped over.
And he took us.
He said, there's nothing here to do.
Just come to, to Krakow.
And I have already there an apartment.
And you'll stay with us.
He, his wife was my friend.
And you'll stay with us.
And you'll see what we can do.
So he was with a horse and buggy and all this and some belongings.
And he took me there, his house.
And then we remained in Krakow till [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till was time to-- until '46.
Yeah.
In Kra-- it was things that they did little bit of business.
It isn't really legitimate, was sort of, you know, here and there.
But-- and then we got a little bit, you know, from the, I think was the from the [INAUDIBLE].
It was like the United Jewish Welfare something.
But there wasn't really-- just a minute.
No, no, no.
I'm confusing.
This was in Vienna.
In Krakow, just, you know, little business buying something, selling cigarettes, things, and this, and to survive.
But we didn't get-- but then an uncle of my got to know that we are alive.
So somehow, he managed to send us-- no.
This is again no.
This was already in Vienna.
In Krakow, we just, just survived just like that, like I'm saying.
You mentioned Vienna.
When did you go there?
To Vienna, we left Krakow in '46.
They again started, you know, making little pogroms, you know, around Krakow, you know.
There were a few Jews killed here and there, in Belz, I think.
And so everybody must, you know-- Jewish people start leaving.
So of course, everybody went west, you know, to Germany.
So we had to go through-- first of all, we had to go through the, the border which was not legitimate.
They took us to the border.
And we went through.
And we went to-- we came to Czechoslovakia, I think, and then from Czechoslovakia to Vienna.
How and what, I can't remember exactly.
But I, I, I remember on big trucks and this.
And then we came to Vienna.
Vienna was like a, like a stopping point for [INAUDIBLE].
And there, we-- everybody stayed for a few days or week or two until they got a big transport.
And they, they took to Germany.
There was a Jewish, Jewish, already organization.
I don't know.
It was [INAUDIBLE], was like the, like the stopping point there.
And then they tried to organ-- to, to, to, to gather enough people and send them to Salzburg and from Salzburg to, to Germany, to these DP-- to these camps.
But when I came there, I found already some friends who came just before me.
And they remained in Vienna.
They didn't want to go to, to Germany because it was already fall.
And they said, it's-- they kept you in these barracks in Linz or some place for quite awhile.
And it's raining and this.
So they said, they remain there.
And also some instead, some before from me to, to, to, to Hungary, to Budapest.
So they came from there.
And when they saw me, you know, coming to [INAUDIBLE], because they used to come there and see who was being brought from, from Poland over there.
So sudden, they saw me there.
And they said, no, you're not going any further.
We'll keep you there.
They didn't have too much where to stay, either.
But they kept us there, they said.
And then you will remain here.
Somehow, we'll see what we can do.
But don't go to Germany.
So we didn't go.
We stayed in Vienna.
And then we got a little, you know, like a sub leased, you know, little room, and this.

And we stayed until, until the time, until the time came to-- I mean, and here, my relatives started working real, real-- you know, they were very aggressive to do something to bring us here, my uncles, my-- you know, the whole family.
When did you get married?
In Krakow-- see, this is what I left out.
Yeah, in Krakow, '45, and we were there.
So we decided, you know, what's the use to.
So we got married.
And, and then this was going on until we left Krakow.
And then Vienna, and then Vienna, we left in '40-- in 1950.
We came to the States.
While we were in Vienna also, there was not, not much to do.
But at that time, my uncles from here used to send us these care packages, you know, all kind of little food, you know, things.
And, and we used to get a little-- from the [INAUDIBLE].
I think it was the [INAUDIBLE], the Jewish like, like the UJF [INAUDIBLE], certain things, you know, little bits, and then also from the [INAUDIBLE] I guess.
But they used to get like on cards, you know, certain little bits of, of food, you know, like milk and bread or whatever.
We used to get this like the, like the-- [INAUDIBLE].
But it was not much
Yeah, something that was sort of like-- because there was-- you couldn't go and buy, you know.
You just have to-- I mean, you had to pay for it, but just limited amounts, you know.
And, and that was, that was until we, you know, until the time came that, you know, we got the-- you know, we were allowed to get to the States, which was a big, big struggle.
Because there was a quota.
And we had to wait.
And we had to-- and here, my relatives, they really tried very hard.
And they were, you know, assuring that they are-- that we are not going to be on any [INAUDIBLE], that they are taking over the responsibility and everything.
But still, it took four years from '46 till, till 1950.
In January, we left Vienna.
In the United States, we arrived February 27.
This I remember.
We arrived in New Orleans.
We came--
Yeah, my uncle-- he lived in Memphis, Tennessee.
And he came.
And he met us there.
And he had some-- and had some cousins there.
And he took us out from, from this place that we-- and he took us to these cousins.
And we had lunch.
And he was so thrilled.
He was, he was just like a father.
He just-- and were in Memphis, Tennessee, for about-- from the time we arrived from the-- February 28th, I guess, until June.
And we stayed in his house.
And I had already that time the baby.
I forgot this.
I had my daughter in Vienna.
The only one I have, the elder.
Yeah.
And her name is Marlene.
In Vienna-- yeah, you see I, I skipped things.
Yeah, I got pregnant.
You know, it was very, very hard.
You know, it was just-- then they send me from here things for the baby and this.
So when, when we came here, she was exactly two years old.
After her second birthday, we, we came here.
And then yeah-- we lived with my uncle.
My brother, the sons of my brother, I and my husband, and my baby, we all came together here.
Yeah, I wasn't talking about my brother, either.
He was away in Russia.
And he came back in 1945, end of 1945.
This is the, the youngest of the brothers, Herman.
Yeah.
So we stayed with my uncle from-- for three months.
And then we realized there was not much to do there.
And we were a little bit unco-- I mean, we stayed there four people and this.
And I was-- they were very good.
They were just, you know, unbelievable.
But we decided to go to a bigger city, because there was not much to do in Memphis in the city that time.
So we want to go to, to New York.
But he didn't like New York.
He said if you're leaving us he was very upset.
He almost cried.
But if you're leaving-- if you can't decide to stay here-- because he, he told, stay another little while.
And then you get to know the language and this.
We'll put you in some kind of thing, maybe business or something or someplace to work.
And things are going to be all right.
But my husband and my brother didn't-- they said, you know, to a bigger city.
So anyway, it's like you're deciding to leave Memphis.
So you have to go to, to California.
He loved California.
And there were a lot of relatives here, too, that he called up-- another uncle, his brother.
He told him that we are coming and to get-- but I said, I'm not coming anymore to live with anybody, unless I'll have an apartment.
Because we have four people.
And I just-- so my uncle, the one who lives here, promised us that he's going to get a place for us and this.
And then we came to Los Angeles, I think in June.
Yeah, in June we came to Los Angeles, 1950.
And this was the--
When we came here, is again, no matter how much our uncles promised us, but we stayed with this uncle for a month.
But he had some little houses.
And his-- and that time was very difficult to get some place to live.
It was like rent control or whatever.
So anyway, one of his tenants was supposed to move out.
And he said as soon she moves out, he'll get this place.
So that what happened, I think, after a month.
And then my husband, you know, what can he do-- I mean, no language and no.
And he comes from a family, which I guess I mentioned before-- his, his whole family was in, in meat business.
So my uncle took him over to a, to a packing house.
And he got a job there.
And he started working there.
He made very little, but somehow, we got-- and my brother, he got a, a job in a bakery.
And this was going on for, for a few years.
And then in '58, we got our own business.
No.
At that time, I had-- yeah, in between, I had the baby that I brought from there.
Then I had my son in 1952.
I had my son.
And I got pregnant and had my son.
So I had to take care of both kids.
And of course, Cross my brother lived with me, too, with us.
So I didn't work, no.
But then when we got our own business, then I used to go in.
And I used to, you know--
A bakery.
Brown's Grocer and Bakery.
[LAUGHS] So-- and then, then I, whenever it was convenient for me, whenever the kids started school, and whenever I took them to school, I used to go to the business.
And that I has to pick them up and go again to the business.
So I was sort of like a part of the business and of course, a housewife, a mother.
And, and that, I guess, from there, I don't know where we go.
[LAUGHS]
Not really.
I don't have any time to develop hobbies, just a housewife, a mother, a grandmother.
Four-- my, my daughter has two, two daughters.
My son has two, also two-- a boy and a girl.
Yeah.
No, in fact, it is 50 years.
It's more than 50 years after the war. 45, 45 is 20-- it's 51-- yeah, 51.
What kind of-- I can leave a message.
It was a terrible tragedy.
It's hard to explain.
It's hard to talk about it.
And hopefully, they will never experience something like that.
Maybe this, this can help the future generations to, to escape, not to go through this kind of a tragedy.
And what else can I say?
Not much, just, just hard to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's hard to express.
I mean, it's hard to express, you know, what-- I hope they'll have a easier life and a, and a more peaceful world and a, and a happier ones.
So-- I don't know what else.
Thank you.
OK.
You're welcome.
I hope this is going to help in some, some way.
[INAUDIBLE]
OK.
You're welcome.
And thank you for being so nice and helping me out.
I didn't express a lot of things that they were going on, because it's very, very difficult in such a short time.
And certain things were very, very tragic and painful.
Then I left my-- when I lost my parents.
And to know all these things.
And it was very-- I was like, for a couple years, I was like frozen.
I didn't know what's going on.
